Risk Register · Change Studies · Full database backup & restore
◉ PDF Report
Printable reports including dashboard KPIs, ICAO matrix, top risks and full risk register.
◫ CSV Export
Export the risk register as CSV — compatible with Excel, Google Sheets and any SMS tool.
⟳ Backup / Restore
Save the complete database (risks + change studies) as JSON. Restore on any device at any time.
📤 Export → CMS
COMPLIANCE MONITORING SYSTEM
Exporte le registre de risques dans le format JSON attendu par le SAF Compliance Monitoring System. Importable via le bouton « 📥 Importer depuis SMS » dans le CMS (onglet Registre & CAP).
◈ Risk ↔ Change Study Links
Risks linked to one or more change studies. Link a risk in the Risk Register edit panel → "Linked Change Study" field.

4. Paste your credentials here — saved locally in your browser

⏱ HELICOPTER DELAY PROCEDURE — >30 MIN AFTER ETA
1
Inform the Operations office (if applicable)
2
Check geolocation — is the aircraft still flying?
3
Try to contact the pilot on the radio
4
No radio response → contact on aircraft telephone or personal telephone
5
Still no response → contact TSOPs or any person on board
6
No response → ACTIVATE THE ERP
REMAIN CALM — DO NOT PANIC
Get victims to safety · Alert emergency services · Alert SAF International · Complete this checklist
📋 LIST OF ITEMS TO BE RECOVERED — §10
Has a flight plan been filed?
Transport of dangerous goods on board?
Has emergency services help been called?
GATHER AS MUCH INFORMATION AS POSSIBLE
VICTIMS?
🔥 Fire starting?
⛽ Fuel leak / smell?
IF THE CALLER IS A CREW MEMBER — ASK:
Can you activate the emergency beacon manually?
Is the first aid kit accessible?
⚡ Evacuate and move away from the helicopter if there is a risk of fire — otherwise stay close by
📋 Assess the whole situation and make a precise report
🩺 Attend to the injured as soon as possible — perform first aid if trained
🚁 If necessary, seek shelter and prepare a landing area for the rescue helicopter
12.3 — ACTIVATION LEVELS
LEVEL 1 — ALERT
Event with potentially hazardous impact but without immediate consequences. Team members placed on alert — must be reachable at all times.
LEVEL 2 — CRISIS
Significant impacts on safety of people or property. All Crisis Management Team members called to the Crisis Management Centre immediately.
12.5 — IMMEDIATE MEASURES CHECKLIST
12.7 — CRISIS LOG
👔
14.1 — ACCOUNTABLE MANAGER (ACM)
• Draft releases to the media — appoint a spokesperson. Do not speak to the media himself where possible.
• Ensure activities of the company are maintained if not affected by the event.
• Be at the disposal of the relevant official organisations.
• Contact customers and sub-contracted companies affected by the accident.
• Interface between organisation and press — only relevant and accurate information, without prejudicing the investigation.
• Limit access to offices and headquarters. Ensure media representatives are escorted at all times.
• Check all buildings and areas are secured (Hangars, Ramps, etc.)
🛡
14.2 — SAFETY MANAGER (SM)
• Leads the crisis unit — centralises all documentation and information (casualties, equipment, location).
• Collect data from SMS database regarding previous occurrences and risk assessments.
• Provide analysis on previous occurrences to ACM and Communication Manager.
• Informs authorities. Point of contact for authorities. Establishes relations with the police on scene.
• Supervises media if allowed access to the site.
• Update SMS information after the crisis.
✈
14.3 — NPFO / NPCT — FLIGHT OPERATIONS / CREW TRAINING
• Gather all information relative to the flight: weather reports, pre-flight planning, W&B, fuel planning, passenger manifest.
• Compile crew administrative documents: training files, FTD records, logbook, medical certificates, licences.
• In co-operation with Maintenance Manager, gather technical documents relating to the aircraft.
• If required, select and brief crews to fly investigators, police or authorised persons to the accident site.
🔧
14.4 — PERSON CONTINUING AIRWORTHINESS (NPCA)
• Gather technical documents and maintenance records (CDN, airframe logbook, engine logbook, etc.).
• Gather information on maintenance personnel: aptitude certificates, training history, complete list.
• Provide technical support to the crisis unit. Select a competent engineer to assist the investigation.
• Help organise the recovery of the wreckage once released by the investigation authority.
🛤
14.5bis — NPGO — GROUND OPERATIONS
• Coordinate and supervise ground operations activities at the accident site and at the organisation\'s premises.
• Ensure the security and access control of hangars, ramps and all operational areas during the crisis.
• Liaise with ground handling service providers and external support teams as required.
• Provide logistical support to crisis unit members and emergency services at the scene.
💼
14.7 — BUSINESS UNIT SPO
• Contact customers affected by the accident, in accordance with current contracts.
• Contact service providers affected by the accident, if necessary.
👥
14.5 — HUMAN RESOURCES
• Inform staff of situation and action to take.
• Provide HR documents relating to involved staff.
• Ensure condolences offered to next of kin.
• Ensure emotional trauma of personnel is addressed — contact psychologists if necessary.
• Manage interface with families of victims.
⚖
14.6 — CFO
• Contact lawyer. Review all statements before circulation.
• Advise on legal issues. Contact insurance companies.
☢
14.8 — DANGEROUS GOODS
• Provide authorities immediately with the list of any dangerous goods that may have been taken away.
1. INTRODUCTION
This Emergency Response Plan was developed based on ORO.GEN.200 and AMC1 ORO.GEN.200(a)(3) and OMM Section 8. It is designed to help the organisation to respond in the case of accidents, serious incidents or any other abnormal event triggering a crisis.
The number of phases that need to be implemented must be established to prevent the potential confusion that can result when emergency situations arise. Senior management must identify the responsibilities within their organisation, respond to the needs of next of kin of the crew and passengers, and provide assistance to the emergency services and the authority in charge of any investigation.
2. AIM OF THE MANUAL
The aim of this Emergency Response Planning (ERP) manual is to:
• Highlight the policies and procedures to be implemented in case of a crisis
• Offer advice to the members of the crisis management team in carrying out their responsibilities
• Communicate relevant information to employees of the organisation and members of the public
The ERP manual is designed to cover crisis situations which cannot specifically or precisely be defined. An organisational framework is presented; however, adaptability and flexibility should be demonstrated in the handling of such events.
3. AMENDMENTS
This manual will be subject to change. Whenever a change is implemented, the Safety Manager shall inform all members of the Crisis Management Team and any person who may be called on to play a role in case of an emergency.
4. EVENTS ACTIVATING THE ERP
✦ Aviation accident / Serious incident
✦ Disaster on premises: fire, explosion, pollution, flood
✦ Loss of working resource: workshop, offices, hangar, aircraft
✦ Impacts of a disaster within the vicinity of the establishment
✦ Climatic event: snow, storm, flood, lightning
✦ Natural disaster: earthquake, volcanic eruption
✦ Food poisoning, epidemic
✦ Death, suicide at the workplace
✦ Multiple victims — disaster, illness or contagion
✦ Accident to public transportation of personnel
✦ Social movements: strike, blocking of accesses
✦ Internal/external threat: attack, bomb, sabotage, terrorism
✦ Loss of energy / communication: electricity, internet, phones
✦ Major media event
✦ Accident during missions: business trip, abroad
5. DEFINITIONS
ACCIDENT
Occurrence between the time any person boards the aircraft until all have disembarked, in which: a person is fatally or seriously injured; OR the aircraft sustains damage or structural failure affecting structural strength or flight characteristics; OR the aircraft is missing or completely inaccessible.
INCIDENT
Occurrence other than an accident, associated with the operation of an aircraft, which affects or could affect the safety of operation.
SERIOUS INCIDENT
An incident involving circumstances indicating that an accident nearly occurred.
FATAL INJURY
Injury resulting in death within 30 days of the date of the accident.
SERIOUS INJURY
Hospitalisation >48h, bone fracture (not fingers/toes/nose), severe lacerations, internal organ injury, burns >5% of body surface, or exposure to infectious substances.
6. ORGANISATION
The Accountable Manager as a single point of contact will be responsible for disseminating the alert to the company personnel and the relevant official authorities. The nominated contact must have immediate access to:
• Emergency checklists to cover the nature of the event
• An up-to-date list of managers to be contacted and their deputies (schedule of on-call executives)
• A list of emergency services and official organisations to be contacted in the event of an emergency
Wherever possible, normal activity must be maintained. Personnel not involved in the management of the situation must not contact the Crisis Management Team or speak to the media. If an employee\'s presence is not required at the accident site or at the Crisis Management Centre, they should be discouraged from going to these locations so as not to hinder the emergency services and/or investigation teams.
8. REACTION TO AN EMERGENCY CALL
Whenever the Company is made aware of an accident or incident, the person or department that receives the alert must endeavour to establish the following information:
• Date and time of the call
• Name and contact details of the informant
• Establish the authenticity of the call (where possible)
• If the call is made anonymously: try to obtain information about the other party and their position; where possible, try to record the conversation and listen to background noise
• If the call is being made from overseas: check the location of the call with the embassy of the country in question
• Initiate the alert process both in-house and externally
8.1 — YOU WITNESSED THE ACCIDENT OR ARE IN CONTACT WITH A WITNESS
REMAIN CALM — DO NOT PANIC
If you witness the event, try not to let your emotions get the better of you and follow these steps:
• Get the victims to safety
• Alert emergency services (cf. § 9 — Emergency numbers)
• Provide assistance and comfort to the best of your ability
• Alert or have SAF International alerted
• Complete the checklist as fully as possible
12.1 — COMPOSITION OF THE CRISIS MANAGEMENT CENTRE
ACM and/or deputy
NPCT and/or deputy
SM and/or deputy
NPGO and/or deputy
NPFO and/or deputy
NPCA
Human Resources
12.2 — MODES OF ACTIVATING THE CRISIS MANAGEMENT CENTRE
ACCIDENT ON COMPANY PREMISES
Any person who witnesses an accident on the organisation\'s premises should:
• Alert SAF International designated personnel (Chapter 9)
• Alert the emergency services (where appropriate)
• Protect any victims/casualties
• Where qualified, apply basic first aid while awaiting emergency services
ACCIDENT OUTSIDE COMPANY PREMISES — CASE 1
The call is received on the SAF URGENCE number. The On-Call Manager informs the ACM (or any person designated in §9), who decides whether or not to activate the Crisis Management Centre.
ACCIDENT OUTSIDE COMPANY PREMISES — CASE 2
The call is received by an employee. The employee contacts the ACM (or any person designated in §9), who decides whether or not to activate the Crisis Management Centre.
12.4 — MAINTAINING A NORMAL SITUATION
To maintain a normal situation as much as possible during ERP activation, several key principles should be followed:
RIGOROUS PREPARATION & TRAINING
Personnel must be thoroughly trained and conduct regular drills. In-depth knowledge of procedures and roles enables calm and orderly reactions.
CLEAR & STRUCTURED COMMUNICATION
Use a dedicated channel, avoid information overload. Clarity and brevity in messages are crucial during emergencies.
DISCRETE PLAN ACTIVATION
ERP activation should be carried out discreetly to avoid creating panic among passengers, uninvolved personnel and non-essential actors.
COORDINATION WITH AUTHORITIES
Coordination with emergency services (firefighters, police, civil aviation authorities) is critical to prevent responses that might exacerbate the emergency.
ADHERENCE TO HIERARCHY & ROLES
The distribution of responsibilities should be respected so that everyone knows precisely their tasks without confusion or overlap.
CONTINUOUS ASSESSMENT & ADAPTATION
Emergencies evolve rapidly — actions must adapt according to new information while keeping to core ERP principles.
12.5 — STANDARD QUESTIONS AFTER OUTBREAK OF THE CRISIS
Are we communicating? Do we communicate actively or defensively?
What tonality do we suggest?
Who are the internal and external stakeholders? How can they be prioritised?
What are the messages for all stakeholders? What messages go only to individual stakeholders?
Which channels are appropriate — active vs. reactive?
In what order and how often must stakeholders be informed (schedule)?
What reactions do we trigger with the chosen form of communication (anticipation)?
Which feedback channels must/can we open?
EXTERNAL STAKEHOLDERS
Customers & Owners
Local, regional & national media
Blue light organisations (police, fire, ambulance)
Local, cantonal & national governments
Administrations & parties
Social partners & suppliers
COMMUNICATION CHANNELS
Media / Social Media Internet (crisis website) Mail / Phone / Fax Hotline
13.1 — COORDINATION WITH OTHER ORGANISATIONS' ERPs
When the activation of SAF International\'s ERP impacts or overlaps with the ERP of another organisation, effective coordination is essential. The following steps are recommended:
IMMEDIATE COMMUNICATION & COORDINATION
Notify the other company\'s crisis management team immediately via a pre-established emergency contact point. Share details about the incident, deployed resources, and areas of intervention.
JOINT SITUATION ASSESSMENT
Crisis teams from both companies should conduct a joint assessment to ensure that response actions do not overlap or create additional risks. Real-time information sharing is essential.
RESOURCE SHARING & MUTUAL SUPPORT
When feasible, consider pooling response resources (rescue teams, equipment) to avoid overloading local infrastructure.
COMPLIANCE WITH REGULATORY PROCEDURES
Civil aviation authorities must be informed of the situation and the ongoing inter-company coordination.
POST-INCIDENT REVIEW & ERP ENHANCEMENT
Once resolved, both companies should conduct a joint post-incident review to identify improvements for their respective ERPs.
13.2 — COMMUNICATION TO THE AUTHORITY
Following an accident, the Safety Manager must inform the authorities as soon as possible of:
Helicopter: type, model, serial number, registration
Names of owner, operator, crew members, customers
Date and time of accident/incident
Location: GPS coordinates, position relative to geographical point
Number of people who died or were injured
Validated passenger list (within 2 hours max)
Description of accident/incident and material damage
Access routes and constraints, physical characteristics of the area
Description of immediate actions taken and planned
List of dangerous goods on board and special passenger categories
13.3 — INTERNAL COMMUNICATION
In responding to an emergency situation, all or part of the organisation\'s personnel may be affected by the event. The Accountable Manager must:
• Make a concerted effort, by whatever means appropriate, to inform all personnel regarding the event
• In a timely manner inform personnel of the steps to be taken for managing the crisis
• Reinforce the policy banning disclosure of information outside the organisation, and the policy for dealing with the media
• Place restrictions on personnel to prevent them from attending the accident location or the CMC unless required within the scope of their duties
Volunteers may be sought to assist families and friends of the victims — it is desirable to have a pre-selected list of volunteers and a list of specialists (psychologists, counsellors) who may be contacted to provide professional assistance.
13.4 — EXTERNAL COMMUNICATION
In the event of a major event, the organisation must implement a detailed communication plan under the guidance of the Legal Department or a lawyer appointed for that purpose.
• Once the CMC has been manned, an initial press release should be disseminated to prevent saturation of lines of communication
• Press releases must not contain the names of persons involved in the accident/incident unless definitively established
13.5 — PRESS CONFERENCE CHECKLIST
PREPARATION
INVITATION
ORGANISATION
13.6 — COMMUNICATION PRINCIPLES
⚡
SPEED
Inform quickly and comprehensively
🔍
OPENNESS
Transparency ensures credibility
📋
COMPLETENESS
No information in slices
🤝
PARTICIPATION
Involve the media in crisis management
15. RETURN TO A NORMAL SITUATION
15.1
Post-incident assessment
Ensure no residual threat is present and all potential risks are managed. Teams on the ground and in the air are involved.
15.2
Coordination with stakeholders
Regular briefings must be held to ensure everyone involved understands the situation and how to proceed.
15.3
Assessment of corrective measures
Any anomalies detected must be corrected and critical equipment tested. Replace or repair elements to avoid any future risk.
15.4
Checking equipment & infrastructure
Inspect all equipment and infrastructure involved to ensure no damage occurred during the intervention.
15.5
Debriefing and feedback
Debriefing with all parties — share lessons learned, identify areas for improvement. Document all feedback to update procedures.
15.6
Gradual resumption of operations
Return to normal operations must be gradual. Prioritise critical flights, then gradually increase to full capacity.
15.7
Support for staff & passengers
Staff affected by the event must be given medical and psychological care.
